name: Kamadeva
description: Kamadeva, the Hindu god of love, desire, and attraction, is a prominent figure in Hindu mythology. Also known as Madana, Manmatha, and Rati-pati (husband of Rati), he embodies the physical, emotional, and sensual aspects of love. He is often depicted as a handsome young man with green or golden skin, wielding a bow made of sugarcane and strung with a line of honeybees, and arrows adorned with five fragrant flowers: ashoka, white lotus, blue lotus, jasmine, and mango blossom. These arrows represent the five senses and the five stages of love. Kamadeva's origins vary across different scriptures. Some accounts portray him as a son of Brahma, the creator, while others identify him as a manifestation of Vishnu or an emanation from Dharma. His primary role is to ignite desire and passion, thus ensuring the continuation of creation. He is believed to be capable of influencing the minds and hearts of all beings, including gods and demons. A significant episode in Kamadeva's mythology involves his attempt to awaken Shiva from his deep meditation after Sati's death. Kamadeva shot his arrow at Shiva, disrupting his penance, but Shiva, enraged, burned Kamadeva to ashes with his third eye. Later, upon the pleas of Rati, Kamadeva was reborn as Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini, thus continuing his role in the cosmic order, albeit in a different form. Kamadeva represents the essential role of love and desire in maintaining the balance and continuity of the universe.
subtitle: God of Love, Desire, and Passion

keyMyths.title: The Burning of Kamadeva
keyMyths.description: Kamadeva attempts to awaken Shiva from his grief-stricken meditation, only to be incinerated by Shiva's third eye. This shows the power of divine wrath and the disruptive force of desire.

keyMyths.title: Kamadeva's Rebirth as Pradyumna
keyMyths.description: After being burned to ashes, Kamadeva is reborn as Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini. This signifies the cyclical nature of life and the persistence of love and desire.

keyMyths.title: Kamadeva and Rati
keyMyths.description: Rati's devotion and pleas to Shiva lead to Kamadeva's eventual rebirth, highlighting the power of love and devotion in overcoming even divine wrath.
